h1Title: Vitamin K Deficiency: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Vitamin K deficiency is a coagulation disorder caused by inadequate availability of vitamin K — a fat-soluble vitamin essential for the gamma-carboxylation (activation) of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X, as well as anticoagulant proteins C and S. Clinically, deficiency manifests as a bleeding diathesis with easy bruising, mucosal bleeding (epistaxis, gingival bleeding, menorrhagia), prolonged prothrombin time (PT/INR), and in severe cases, intracranial or gastrointestinal hemorrhage. The condition results from inadequate dietary intake, fat malabsorption, cholestatic liver disease, prolonged antibiotic use disrupting gut flora, or newborn hemorrhagic disease. Treatment with oral or intravenous phytonadione (vitamin K1) rapidly corrects the defect — PT normalizes within 24-48 hours in most patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Vitamin K deficiency affects 10-20% of hospitalized patients; approximately 5-10% of newborns have biochemical evidence of deficiency at birth; the condition is underdiagnosed in adults with unexplained bleeding or elevated INR
keyStats.demographics: Newborns (particularly premature) and adults over 60 are at highest risk; patients with fat malabsorption, biliary disease, or prolonged antibiotic use are most susceptible regardless of age
keyStats.avgAge: Biochemical deficiency peaks in the neonatal period (first week of life) and in adults over 65 with chronic disease and polypharmacy
keyStats.globalCases: Subclinical vitamin K deficiency is common in populations with limited green leafy vegetable consumption; overt bleeding complications are more frequent in hospitalized and institutionalized patients

definition: Vitamin K deficiency (ICD-10: E56.1) is a disorder of hemostasis resulting from insufficient vitamin K to support the gamma-carboxylation of coagulation proteins synthesized in the liver. Vitamin K exists in two principal forms: K1 (phylloquinone), found in green leafy vegetables (kale, spinach, broccoli, lettuce) and vegetable oils; and K2 (menaquinones), produced by gut Bacteroides and other anaerobic bacteria and also present in fermented foods (natto, cheese, egg yolk). The gamma-carboxylation step is catalyzed by the enzyme vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKOR); warfarin exerts its anticoagulant effect by inhibiting VKOR and blocking the recycling of vitamin K. Deficiency produces a selective reduction in functional (gamma-carboxylated) factors II, VII, IX, and X — creating the characteristic pattern of prolonged PT (measuring the extrinsic pathway, where factor VII is rate-limiting) with relatively preserved activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT). The prothrombin induced by deficiency (PIVKA-II, protein induced by vitamin K absence) is non-functional and serves as a diagnostic marker of subclinical deficiency.

typesClassification.description: Insufficient intake of phylloquinone (K1) from green leafy vegetables — the classic cause in elderly patients with poor nutrition, anorexia, or institutionalized individuals with limited access to fresh vegetables. Often combined with fat malabsorption reducing absorption of fat-soluble K1.

Conditions that impair fat absorption — celiac disease, Crohn's disease, pancreatic insufficiency, bariatric surgery, and cholestatic liver disease — reduce absorption of fat-soluble vitamin K1 from the diet. K2 production by gut bacteria may also be impaired by broad-spectrum antibiotics disrupting the microbiome.

Prolonged or repeated courses of broad-spectrum antibiotics (particularly fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins, and tetracyclines) eliminate vitamin K2-producing gut bacteria, reducing the K2 contribution to total vitamin K status. Risk is highest in malnourished patients and those on multiple antibiotic courses.

Newborns are born with depleted vitamin K stores due to poor placental transfer of vitamin K and sterile gut flora (no gut bacteria producing K2). The classic presentation is umbilical cord bleeding, gastrointestinal bleeding, or intracranial hemorrhage within the first week of life — preventable with vitamin K prophylaxis at birth.

Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKOR), creating a functional vitamin K deficiency state by blocking the regeneration of the active hydroquinone form. This is the intended anticoagulant effect but can progress to bleeding if over-anticoagulated or if dietary vitamin K intake is erratic.

Biliary obstruction and cholestatic liver disease impair fat absorption and vitamin K uptake; combined with reduced hepatic synthesis of clotting factors, this creates a bleeding tendency from both reduced gamma-carboxylated factor levels and reduced factor production.

primarySymptoms: Easy bruising and ecchymoses on the limbs, trunk, or face — spontaneous or minimal-trauma bruising in a patient with otherwise unremarkable medical history is a classic early sign of vitamin K deficiency.

Mucosal bleeding including epistaxis (nosebleeds), gingival bleeding during tooth brushing, and hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum) — bleeding from mucous membranes reflects the systemic coagulation defect.
Menorrhagia (heavy menstrual bleeding) in women of reproductive age — patients who previously had normal menstrual flows may notice substantially heavier periods and prolonged bleeding days.
Prolonged bleeding after minor cuts or dental procedures — bleeding continues longer than expected from small wounds, a hallmark of the clotting factor deficiency.

Gastrointestinal bleeding manifesting as melena (black, tarry stools) or hematemesis (vomiting blood) — upper GI bleeding can be severe in advanced deficiency, presenting as coffee-ground vomit or melena.

Intracranial hemorrhage presenting with headache, vomiting, neurological deficits, or altered consciousness — a life-threatening complication most common in newborns with hemorrhagic disease and in adults with advanced deficiency receiving anticoagulation.
Bleeding from injection sites — patients on anticoagulants with underlying vitamin K deficiency may develop hematomas at injection sites or prolonged bleeding after phlebotomy.

Bleeding into muscles or joints (hemarthrosis) — less common in vitamin K deficiency than in hemophilia, but severe deficiency with concurrent anticoagulation or trauma can produce joint or muscle hemorrhage.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained elevation in PT/INR without warfarin use — an elevated PT in a non-anticoagulated patient, particularly one with malabsorption, liver disease, or recent antibiotics, is a key warning sign
Spontaneous bruising without trauma history — any new ecchymosis pattern that appears without a clear inciting event should prompt coagulation testing

Bleeding that is disproportionate to the degree of anticoagulation — e.g., a patient on a modest warfarin dose with INR of 2.5 bleeding heavily suggests underlying vitamin K deficiency amplifying the anticoagulant effect
New menorrhagia in a woman with cholestasis or fat malabsorption — hormonal changes in menstruation combined with coagulopathy can produce severe menstrual bleeding
Poor dietary intake combined with antibiotic use — elderly patients with minimal vegetable consumption who receive a prolonged antibiotic course are at high risk and should be monitored with PT/INR
emergencySigns: Sudden severe headache with vomiting, seizures, or focal neurological deficits — intracranial hemorrhage is a medical emergency requiring immediate neuroimaging and urgent hematology involvement
Massive gastrointestinal bleeding with hemodynamic instability — melena or hematemesis with hypotension, tachycardia, and falling hemoglobin requires urgent resuscitation and endoscopy
Bleeding into the airway (massive hemoptysis or hematemesis) — airway protection is the immediate priority, followed by correction of coagulopathy with vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma
Spontaneous hemarthrosis with severe joint pain and swelling — joint hemorrhage requires urgent factor replacement and vitamin K administration; differential includes hemophilia
Multiple large ecchymoses with hemodynamic instability — severe bleeding into soft tissues can cause hypovolemia and requires urgent factor replacement alongside vitamin K

causes.description: Conditions impairing fat absorption — celiac disease, Crohn's disease, pancreatic insufficiency, small bowel resection, and severe steatorrhea — reduce absorption of fat-soluble vitamin K1 from dietary sources. Bile salts are required for K1 absorption; cholestasis and biliary obstruction therefore impair uptake significantly. The combination of fat malabsorption and limited dietary K1 creates deficiency more rapidly than either alone.

Vitamin K2 (menaquinones) are synthesized by anaerobic gut bacteria, particularly Bacteroides fragilis and other Bacteroides species. Broad-spectrum antibiotics that disrupt the gut microbiome reduce endogenous K2 production by 50-80%, creating a functional deficiency particularly in patients with borderline dietary K1 intake. Fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins, and clindamycin are particularly implicated.

Low consumption of green leafy vegetables (the primary K1 source) is the classic cause of dietary vitamin K deficiency. Elderly patients, those with anorexia, institutionalized individuals, and patients with restricted diets (e.g., dialysis patients with potassium restriction limiting leafy greens) are at risk. The recommended daily intake of 90-120 mcg/day is rarely achieved in these populations.

Biliary obstruction and cholestatic liver disease (primary sclerosing cholangitis, primary biliary cholangitis, biliary atresia) impair fat-soluble vitamin absorption. Combined with reduced hepatic synthesis of clotting factors due to synthetic liver dysfunction, cholestatic patients develop bleeding from multiple mechanisms simultaneously.

Newborns are functionally vitamin K deficient at birth due to: minimal placental transfer of vitamin K (maternal K1 crosses the placenta poorly), sterile gut flora (no endogenous K2 production in the first days of life), and low vitamin K content of breast milk. This physiological deficiency is exacerbated in premature infants. Bleeding manifestations appear in the first week of life (classical HDN) or later (late-onset HDN at 2-12 weeks, often associated with breastfeeding and occult cholestasis).

Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKOR), blocking the recycling of vitamin K to its active form. This creates a functional vitamin K deficiency state in all patients taking warfarin. Bleeding occurs when the degree of VKOR inhibition (measured by INR) exceeds what can be compensated by residual vitamin K from diet and gut flora. Risk is highest with dietary K1 fluctuations, drug interactions (antibiotics, azole antifungals, amiodarone), and over-anticoagulation.

riskFactors.description: Celiac disease, Crohn's disease, chronic pancreatitis, and bariatric surgery dramatically reduce vitamin K absorption and are among the most common causes of deficiency in non-hospitalized adults.

Four or more weeks of broad-spectrum antibiotics, especially fluoroquinolones or cephalosporins, can reduce gut K2 production by 50-80% and precipitate deficiency in patients with borderline dietary K1 intake.
Elderly patients have reduced dietary K1 intake, higher rates of malabsorption, more antibiotic exposure, and more polypharmacy (including warfarin) — the combination creates high vulnerability.

riskFactors.factor: Biliary obstruction or cholestatic liver disease
riskFactors.description: Any condition causing reduced bile flow (gallstones, pancreatic head tumors, primary sclerosing cholangitis, primary biliary cholangitis) impairs fat-soluble vitamin absorption significantly.

All patients on warfarin are in a functional vitamin K deficiency state. Erratic dietary K1 intake, drug interactions, and over-anticoagulation can push them into clinically significant deficiency with bleeding.
Diets lacking green leafy vegetables provide insufficient phylloquinone. Patients on restricted diets (e.g., some low-oxalate or renal diets) can inadvertently develop low vitamin K status.
Premature infants have even lower vitamin K stores at birth, more immature liver function for factor synthesis, and higher risk of intracranial hemorrhage in the first weeks of life.

CKD patients have reduced dietary intake, altered gut microbiome, and high rates of antibiotic use — contributing to deficiency. Additionally, warfarin use is common in CKD patients with atrial fibrillation.

affectedDemographics: Vitamin K deficiency is most prevalent in hospitalized elderly patients, with 10-20% having biochemical evidence of deficiency (elevated PIVKA-II or PT)
Neonates represent a distinct high-risk group — the incidence of late-onset vitamin K deficiency bleeding is approximately 4-10 per 100,000 births in countries without routine prophylaxis
Adults with fat malabsorption (celiac, Crohn's, chronic pancreatitis) constitute a significant group, often underdiagnosed until bleeding complications occur
Patients on warfarin represent the largest treated group at risk — the margin between therapeutic anticoagulation and bleeding from over-anticoagulation is fundamentally a vitamin K balance question
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of vitamin K deficiency begins with coagulation studies showing isolated prolonged PT (INR elevated) with relatively normal aPTT — this pattern reflects selective deficiency of factors II, VII, IX, and X (vitamin K-dependent factors). Factor VII has the shortest half-life (approximately 6 hours) and declines first, making PT the most sensitive early marker. Serum vitamin K levels can be measured directly but are not widely available and results are slow. PIVKA-II (protein induced by vitamin K absence/antagonist-II) is a more specific marker of functional vitamin K deficiency — it measures the non-gamma-carboxylated form of prothrombin and is elevated in true deficiency even before PT becomes abnormal. In neonates, diagnosis is clinical (bleeding presentation) plus biochemical (prolonged PT/INR, low factor VII assay). The clinical response to vitamin K administration — PT normalizing within 24-48 hours of oral or IV phytonadione — is both therapeutic and diagnostic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary screening test for vitamin K deficiency. PT measures the extrinsic and common pathway (factors VII, X, V, II, fibrinogen). Factor VII is vitamin K-dependent and declines earliest in deficiency, making PT the most sensitive marker. INR elevation disproportionate to clinical anticoagulation suggests deficiency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw (no fasting required). Results available within minutes in hospital labs. Normal PT is 11-13.5 seconds; in deficiency, PT is prolonged above the normal range, and INR may be elevated above 1.0 in non-anticoagulated patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the intrinsic and common pathway. In vitamin K deficiency, aPTT may be normal early (factors XII, XI, IX, VIII are not vitamin K-dependent) but may lengthen as deficiency becomes severe and factors II and X decline significantly.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results within minutes. Less sensitive than PT for early vitamin K deficiency; a normal aPTT with elevated PT is a classic early finding in the condition.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct measurement of functional factor VII activity. Factor VII has the shortest half-life (6 hours) of the vitamin K-dependent factors and is the first to fall in deficiency. A low factor VII level with elevated PT confirms vitamin K deficiency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialized coagulation assay performed in a hematology laboratory. Results available in 1-2 days. Factor VII activity below 50% of normal indicates significant deficiency.

diagnosticTests.test: PIVKA-II (protein induced by vitamin K absence-II)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The most specific marker of functional vitamin K deficiency — measures non-gamma-carboxylated prothrombin (des-gamma-carboxy prothrombin) in the blood. Elevated PIVKA-II is detectable even before PT becomes abnormal, allowing for subclinical diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw sent to a specialized coagulation laboratory. Results in several days. PIVKA-II is particularly useful for diagnosing deficiency in newborns and for detecting subclinical deficiency in at-risk adults.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct measurement of circulating vitamin K1 levels by HPLC with electrochemical detection. Normal range is 0.2-3.2 ng/mL. Levels below 0.2 ng/mL indicate deficiency. However, levels do not always correlate with coagulation status because functional deficiency can occur with low-normal vitamin K in the presence of warfarin.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting blood draw required (vitamin K is fat-soluble and levels fluctuate with dietary intake). Sent to specialized labs. Results in 1-2 weeks. Not widely available; used primarily in research and complex diagnostic cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Functional assessment of clot formation and lysis — useful in assessing the overall hemostatic capacity in vitamin K deficiency, particularly in bleeding patients or those requiring surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Whole blood test, results within 30 minutes. TEG/ROTEM shows prolonged clotting time and reduced clot strength in vitamin K deficiency. Used primarily in surgical and trauma settings.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of vitamin K deficiency depends on severity and clinical context. For mild-to-moderate deficiency without active bleeding: oral phytonadione (vitamin K1) 5-10 mg daily for 7-14 days is effective, with PT normalizing within 24-48 hours. For moderate deficiency with bleeding or INR above 4.0: intravenous phytonadione is preferred because IV administration provides rapid and complete delivery — PT corrects within 6-24 hours. Oral vitamin K is slower (24-72 hours) and less reliable if malabsorption is present. For severe bleeding with hemodynamic compromise: IV vitamin K (10-20 mg) plus fresh frozen plasma (FFP) or prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) provides both rapid factor replacement and sustained correction. For neonatal hemorrhagic disease: intramuscular vitamin K (0.5-1 mg phytonadione) at birth is the universal prophylaxis that has dramatically reduced incidence; for established bleeding, IV vitamin K 1-2 mg is given with FFP or PCC if bleeding is severe. Dietary supplementation with leafy green vegetables should accompany pharmacologic treatment to prevent recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: Phytonadione (vitamin K1) oral 5-10 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Oral vitamin K1 for mild-to-moderate deficiency without active bleeding. Effective in patients with adequate gut absorption. Dose: 5-10 mg orally daily for 7-14 days until PT normalizes. Can be used for maintenance in patients with chronic fat malabsorption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects PT within 24-48 hours in most patients with adequate intestinal absorption. Well tolerated with no significant adverse effects at these doses. Lower doses (1-2 mg) are used for warfarin reversal when the goal is partial correction to therapeutic INR.

medicalTreatments.name: Phytonadione (vitamin K1) IV 10-20 mg
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous vitamin K1 for moderate-to-severe deficiency, active bleeding, or INR above 4.0. IV administration bypasses gut absorption entirely and achieves complete and rapid delivery. Must be given slowly (over 30-60 minutes) to avoid anaphylactoid reactions (flushing, chest discomfort, hypotension).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PT normalizes within 6-24 hours in most patients. The most reliable route in patients with fat malabsorption or when rapid correction is needed. Anaphylactoid reactions occur in approximately 3 in 10,000 doses — administer in a monitored setting.

medicalTreatments.name: Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) 4-factor
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous concentrate containing factors II, VII, IX, and X. Used for severe bleeding (ICH, major GI bleed) with vitamin K deficiency — provides immediate factor replacement while vitamin K takes effect. Preferred over FFP for faster correction, smaller volume, and more predictable factor levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate normalization of INR and PT upon infusion. Lasts until the vitamin K (given concurrently) takes effect (6-24 hours). Dosing: 25-50 units/kg IV depending on baseline INR. Significantly faster and more reliable than FFP.

medicalTreatments.name: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP)
medicalTreatments.description: Plasma from donated blood containing all clotting factors. Used when PCC is unavailable for severe bleeding. Requires large volumes (15-30 mL/kg) and blood type matching — slower and less precise than PCC.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides temporary factor replacement until vitamin K takes effect. Volume overload is a concern in elderly patients. Largely superseded by PCC in modern practice for vitamin K deficiency bleeding.

medicalTreatments.name: Phytonadione IM (newborn prophylaxis)
medicalTreatments.description: Intramuscular vitamin K1 0.5 mg (for preterm) or 1 mg (for term) given at birth — the WHO and AAP-recommended universal prophylaxis that has reduced neonatal hemorrhagic disease by 90%. A single IM dose at birth provides protection for the first 6-8 weeks of life.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Virtually eliminates early and classical vitamin K deficiency bleeding. The injection can be given simultaneously with vitamin K1 IV for severe neonatal bleeding. Oral vitamin K is an alternative but requires multiple doses and has lower effectiveness.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary supplementation with phylloquinone (K1)
alternativeTreatments.description: Increasing green leafy vegetable intake (spinach, kale, broccoli, lettuce) to maintain adequate K1 status. Recommended as adjunct to treatment and for prevention of recurrence. 100-200 mcg daily (equivalent to one serving of cooked spinach or kale) is sufficient for maintenance.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents recurrence in patients with dietary deficiency. Less effective in patients with fat malabsorption or cholestasis, who require continued supplementation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Menaquinone-7 (vitamin K2 MK-7) supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: K2 supplements (particularly menaquinone-7 from natto or supplements) are being studied for bone health and vascular calcification prevention in addition to coagulation. K2 may have a longer half-life than K1 and potentially better tissue distribution.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for coagulation support when K1 is poorly absorbed. 90-180 mcg daily of MK-7 is typical. The evidence base for coagulation is less robust than for K1, but K2 supplementation is a reasonable adjunct especially in patients with fat malabsorption.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic supplementation to restore gut flora
alternativeTreatments.description: Probiotic formulations containing Bacteroides species and other K2-producing bacteria may restore endogenous vitamin K2 production in patients with antibiotic-induced deficiency. Particularly relevant in patients on repeated antibiotic courses.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate benefit for restoring gut K2 production in antibiotic-associated deficiency. More research needed to establish optimal strains and dosing. Not a substitute for oral/IV vitamin K in acute deficiency.

whySeeSpecialist: A hematologist should be involved in managing active bleeding from vitamin K deficiency, particularly when factor replacement (PCC/FFP) is needed. A gastroenterologist is appropriate when the underlying cause is fat malabsorption, cholestatic liver disease, or biliary obstruction. An endocrinologist can address the broader vitamin and mineral status in patients with chronic deficiency. In newborns with hemorrhagic disease, a pediatric hematologist and neonatologist should be involved. A dietitian can provide structured guidance on increasing dietary vitamin K intake.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a hematologist experienced in coagulation disorders — they can manage both the acute bleeding episode and the investigation of underlying causes (fat malabsorption, gut flora disruption, cholestatic liver disease). For patients with chronic conditions causing malabsorption, a gastroenterologist should investigate the underlying enteric or hepatobiliary disease. Ask about PIVKA-II testing availability and access to specialized coagulation labs.
preventionStrategies: Universal vitamin K prophylaxis at birth (0.5-1 mg IM) eliminates 90% of neonatal hemorrhagic disease — this is the single most effective preventive measure in medicine
Patients on prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics should receive prophylactic oral vitamin K1 (2-5 mg daily) to prevent deficiency from gut flora depletion, particularly if dietary K1 intake is low

Patients with fat malabsorption (celiac, Crohn's, chronic pancreatitis) and cholestatic liver disease should receive regular vitamin K1 supplementation (1-2 mg daily orally or IM monthly) with PT/INR monitoring

Warfarin patients should maintain consistent dietary vitamin K intake rather than avoiding leafy greens entirely — erratic intake creates INR instability more dangerous than moderate, consistent intake
lifestyleModifications: Incorporate at least one serving of green leafy vegetables daily — spinach, kale, broccoli, lettuce, bok choy, and collard greens are rich in phylloquinone (K1)
Include fermented foods (natto, cheese, yogurt) regularly to support gut-derived vitamin K2 production
Avoid excessive supplementation of vitamins A and E — high doses of these fat-soluble vitamins antagonize vitamin K metabolism and can precipitate deficiency
If taking warfarin, maintain a consistent intake of vitamin K-rich foods rather than fluctuating — sudden increases in K1 intake reduce warfarin effect while sudden decreases can cause INR elevation
Patients with fat malabsorption should work with a dietitian to optimize fat intake timing with vitamin K supplementation for better absorption

recommended: Green leafy vegetables (spinach, kale, collard greens, Swiss chard, broccoli, lettuce) — one serving daily provides sufficient phylloquinone for maintenance; cooked greens have higher K1 density per serving than raw
Natto (fermented soybeans) — one of the richest natural sources of vitamin K2 (menaquinone-7); 100g provides approximately 1000 mcg of K2, far exceeding daily requirements
Fermented dairy (cheese, yogurt, kefir) — moderate K2 content; cheese (particularly hard cheeses like gouda and brie) provides 70-80 mcg of K2 per 100g
Egg yolks — modest K2 content; pastured eggs from hens fed green forage have higher K2 levels
Vegetable oils (soybean oil, canola oil) — contain phylloquinone; cooking with these oils contributes to K1 intake
Plant oils (olive oil, avocado oil) used in conjunction with leafy greens — fat improves K1 absorption from the vegetable matrix
avoid: Excessive vitamin A supplements (above 10,000 IU daily) — vitamin A antagonizes vitamin K metabolism and can worsen bleeding tendency
Excessive vitamin E supplements (above 400 IU daily) — high-dose vitamin E inhibits vitamin K-dependent carboxylase and can precipitate bleeding
Avoiding all leafy greens 'to protect from warfarin' — this creates severe dietary K1 deficiency that destabilizes INR more than moderate intake would stabilize it
Orlistat or other fat malabsorption-inducing weight loss drugs — these dramatically reduce vitamin K absorption and are contraindicated in patients with known deficiency

exerciseGuidelines: No specific exercise restrictions for vitamin K deficiency — exercise is beneficial for general health. Patients with bleeding into joints or muscles should avoid contact sports and heavy resistance training until coagulation is normalized. Light aerobic exercise (walking, swimming, cycling) is safe at all stages of treatment and recovery. After resolution, no restrictions are necessary.
dailyManagement: Take oral vitamin K1 as prescribed — typically 5-10 mg daily for 7-14 days for deficiency; consistency matters for normalization of PT
Monitor PT/INR regularly during treatment — check 24-48 hours after starting vitamin K to confirm response; repeat weekly until stable
If on warfarin, inform your hematologist before making any dietary changes — maintaining consistent K1 intake is the goal, not minimizing it
Report any new bruising, nosebleeds, gum bleeding, or black stools to your physician immediately — these may indicate incomplete correction of deficiency
Take vitamin K supplements with a fatty meal (if oral) to optimize absorption — fat stimulates bile release and improves K1 uptake
For patients with chronic malabsorption, work with a gastroenterologist to address the underlying condition while maintaining regular vitamin K supplementation
prognosis: Vitamin K deficiency has an excellent prognosis with prompt treatment — PT normalizes within 24-48 hours of adequate vitamin K administration in most patients, and bleeding stops rapidly. The primary determinant of outcome is the underlying cause: dietary deficiency resolves fully with improved nutrition and oral supplementation; fat malabsorption requires ongoing supplementation; cholestatic liver disease may require higher doses and IV administration. Neonatal hemorrhagic disease has largely been eradicated by routine vitamin K prophylaxis at birth. The key long-term consideration is preventing recurrence — patients with chronic risk factors (fat malabsorption, cholestasis, warfarin use, repeated antibiotics) need ongoing monitoring and maintenance supplementation. The prognosis for acute bleeding episodes (ICH, major GI hemorrhage) depends on the speed of coagulopathy correction and the extent of the hemorrhage; rapid correction with IV vitamin K and PCC/FFP substantially improves survival in these critical situations.
recoveryTimeline: PT typically normalizes within 24-48 hours of starting oral vitamin K1 in patients with dietary deficiency and normal absorption. IV vitamin K produces faster correction (6-24 hours). Factor VII levels normalize within 48-72 hours. Bleeding symptoms (bruising, mucosal bleeding) resolve within days of coagulation correction. Chronic deficiency from fat malabsorption may require longer treatment periods (2-4 weeks) and ongoing maintenance supplementation. Newborns respond rapidly to IM or IV vitamin K, with bleeding stopping within hours.
complications: Intracranial hemorrhage — the most serious complication of vitamin K deficiency, most common in newborns with hemorrhagic disease and in adults with advanced deficiency on anticoagulation; carries high mortality and morbidity even with rapid treatment
Gastrointestinal hemorrhage — massive upper GI bleeding from vitamin K deficiency can cause hemodynamic instability and death; requires urgent endoscopy, factor replacement, and IV vitamin K
Hemarthrosis — bleeding into joints (particularly knee, elbow, ankle) causes acute pain, swelling, and restricted movement; can cause permanent joint damage if recurrent
Retrobulbar hemorrhage — bleeding behind the eye causing proptosis, pain, and vision loss; an ophthalmologic emergency
Post-operative bleeding — patients with undiagnosed vitamin K deficiency are at risk for catastrophic surgical bleeding; preoperative PT/INR screening in high-risk patients prevents this complication

supportResources.name: WHO. Vitamin A supplementation and vitamin K prophylaxis guidelines for newborns
supportResources.description: WHO guidelines on vitamin K prophylaxis in newborns, including dosing, administration routes, and programmatic implementation for national immunization programs.

supportResources.name: American Society of Hematology — Thrombosis and Hemostasis
supportResources.description: Patient education resources on bleeding disorders including vitamin K deficiency, coagulation testing, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: National Hemophilia Foundation
supportResources.description: Resources for patients with bleeding disorders. While focused on hemophilia and von Willebrand disease, the general information about living with a bleeding tendency is applicable.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hemophilia A causes prolonged aPTT (intrinsic pathway) with normal PT; vitamin K deficiency causes prolonged PT with relatively normal aPTT initially. Hemophilia causes hemarthrosis and deep tissue bleeding; vitamin K deficiency causes mucosal bleeding and bruising. Factor VIII levels are normal in vitamin K deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Liver Disease (Coagulopathy of Chronic Liver Disease)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Liver disease causes prolongation of both PT and aPTT (because the liver synthesizes all clotting factors). Vitamin K deficiency causes isolated PT prolongation (factor VII declines first). Liver disease patients may also be vitamin K deficient (overlap), but factor V (not vitamin K-dependent) is low in liver disease and normal in pure vitamin K deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: DIC causes prolonged PT and aPTT plus low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and thrombocytopenia — reflecting consumptive coagulopathy from systemic activation of clotting. Vitamin K deficiency does not cause low fibrinogen, thrombocytopenia, or elevated D-dimer. DIC is a systemic process; vitamin K deficiency is a specific factor deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Warfarin Over-anticoagulation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Warfarin causes elevated PT/INR through VKOR inhibition (functional vitamin K deficiency). The pattern of coagulation abnormality is identical. Distinguishing 'true' vitamin K deficiency from 'warfarin-induced' deficiency requires history, clinical context, and response to vitamin K. In warfarin patients, the goal is to reduce INR to therapeutic range, not to normalize it fully.

coOccurringConditions.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia (from chronic GI bleeding)

relatedConditions.name: Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: NAFLD and cholestatic liver disease are common causes of fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption; NAFLD patients often have poor dietary intake and may develop vitamin K deficiency with associated bleeding complications.

relatedConditions.name: Celiac Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Celiac disease causes villous atrophy and fat malabsorption that dramatically reduces vitamin K absorption. Patients with untreated or poorly controlled celiac disease are at high risk for vitamin K deficiency and should receive supplementation and monitoring.

relatedConditions.name: Cirrhosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Cirrhotic patients have multiple mechanisms of bleeding: reduced clotting factor synthesis (from synthetic liver failure) and reduced vitamin K absorption (from cholestasis and portal hypertension). Both contribute to coagulopathy. Vitamin K supplementation may help but synthetic dysfunction limits its effectiveness.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Pancreatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic pancreatitis causes exocrine insufficiency and fat malabsorption, reducing vitamin K absorption. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy improves fat absorption and vitamin K status, and supplementation should accompany enzyme replacement.

faqs.question: What does vitamin K deficiency do to your body?
faqs.answer: Vitamin K deficiency impairs the body's ability to form blood clots — specifically, it prevents the gamma-carboxylation (activation) of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. Without these activated factors, bleeding is prolonged and may be severe. The clinical result is easy bruising, mucosal bleeding, and in severe cases, life-threatening hemorrhage.

faqs.question: How do you treat vitamin K deficiency?
faqs.answer: Treatment is with phytonadione (vitamin K1): oral doses of 5-10 mg daily for mild deficiency, or IV doses of 10-20 mg for moderate-to-severe deficiency or active bleeding. PT normalizes within 24-48 hours. For severe bleeding, IV vitamin K is given alongside prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) for immediate factor replacement.

faqs.question: What foods are high in vitamin K?
faqs.answer: Green leafy vegetables are the richest source of vitamin K1 (phylloquinone): spinach, kale, collard greens, Swiss chard, broccoli, and lettuce. Natto (fermented soybeans) is extremely high in vitamin K2 (menaquinone-7). Cheese, egg yolks, and fermented dairy products provide moderate amounts of K2.

faqs.question: What is the difference between vitamin K1 and vitamin K2?
faqs.answer: Vitamin K1 (phylloquinone) comes from plants, particularly green leafy vegetables, and is the primary dietary source. Vitamin K2 (menaquinones) is produced by gut bacteria and found in fermented foods. K2 has a longer half-life and better tissue distribution. Both are essential for gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors; K2 also has important roles in bone and cardiovascular health.

faqs.question: Can vitamin K deficiency be fatal?
faqs.answer: Yes — severe vitamin K deficiency can cause life-threatening bleeding including intracranial hemorrhage and massive gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Intracranial hemorrhage in newborns has high mortality. In adults, major bleeding from vitamin K deficiency (particularly with concurrent anticoagulation) is a serious medical emergency.

faqs.question: Why are newborns given vitamin K?
faqs.answer: Newborns receive vitamin K at birth because they are born with depleted vitamin K stores: vitamin K crosses the placenta poorly, breast milk contains very little vitamin K, and newborns have no gut bacteria to produce K2. Without prophylaxis, bleeding (particularly intracranial hemorrhage) occurs in the first weeks of life. A single IM dose at birth eliminates 90% of this risk.

faqs.question: What causes low vitamin K levels?
faqs.answer: The main causes are: inadequate dietary intake (low vegetable consumption), fat malabsorption (celiac disease, Crohn's disease, pancreatitis), cholestatic liver disease, prolonged antibiotic use (disrupts gut K2-producing bacteria), and warfarin anticoagulation. Newborns are inherently deficient at birth.

faqs.question: How do you test for vitamin K deficiency?
faqs.answer: The primary test is PT/INR — prolonged PT with normal aPTT is the classic early finding. Factor VII assay confirms low functional factor VII. PIVKA-II (protein induced by vitamin K absence-II) is a more sensitive test detecting deficiency before PT becomes abnormal. Serum vitamin K level can be measured directly but is not widely available.

faqs.question: Can you have vitamin K deficiency without prolonged PT?
faqs.answer: Yes — subclinical vitamin K deficiency exists before PT becomes abnormal. PIVKA-II (non-gamma-carboxylated prothrombin) is detectable in the blood before PT lengthens. This is why at-risk patients (malabsorption, cholestasis, prolonged antibiotics) should be monitored with PIVKA-II or periodic PT even before bleeding symptoms appear.

faqs.question: Does vitamin K help with bruising?
faqs.answer: Vitamin K corrects the underlying coagulopathy that causes easy bruising in deficiency states. However, once bruising has formed from minor bleeding into the skin, vitamin K accelerates the correction of the bleeding tendency but does not directly speed the resolution of the bruise itself. Bruises fade over 1-2 weeks as the hematoma is metabolized.

faqs.question: Can vitamin K deficiency cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Vitamin K deficiency does not directly cause anemia. However, chronic gastrointestinal bleeding from vitamin K deficiency (e.g., from cholestatic liver disease and malabsorption) can lead to iron deficiency anemia over time. The anemia is from blood loss, not from a clotting factor defect.

faqs.question: Is vitamin K the same as potassium?
faqs.answer: No — vitamin K and potassium are completely different substances. Vitamin K is a fat-soluble vitamin essential for blood clotting. Potassium is a mineral electrolyte (K+) critical for nerve and muscle function. They are not related and have no metabolic interaction.

faqs.question: How much vitamin K do you need daily?
faqs.answer: The adequate intake for vitamin K is 90-120 mcg/day for adults (higher for pregnant and lactating women at 90-75 mcg/day). This is easily achieved from a single serving of cooked leafy greens. The therapeutic dose for deficiency is much higher (5-10 mg, or 5,000-10,000 mcg — about 50-100 times the daily requirement).

faqs.question: Can you take too much vitamin K?
faqs.answer: Vitamin K toxicity is extremely rare — no toxic upper limit has been established for phylloquinone from food or supplements. Even very high doses (hundreds of milligrams) have been used therapeutically without significant toxicity. The main risk is in patients on warfarin, where excessive vitamin K can antagonize anticoagulant effect.

faqs.question: Does vitamin K interact with blood thinners?
faqs.answer: Vitamin K directly antagonizes warfarin (a vitamin K antagonist) — higher vitamin K intake reduces warfarin's anticoagulant effect, while lower intake (or deficiency) potentiates it. Patients on warfarin should maintain consistent dietary vitamin K intake and inform their physician before taking vitamin K supplements. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are not affected by vitamin K.

faqs.question: How long does vitamin K stay in the body?
faqs.answer: Vitamin K1 (phylloquinone) has a short circulating half-life of approximately 1-2 hours. However, its effects on clotting factor gamma-carboxylation persist longer. Vitamin K2 (menaquinone-7) has a much longer half-life (60-70 hours), allowing for less frequent dosing in supplement form.

faqs.question: Does vitamin K help bones?
faqs.answer: Yes — vitamin K is essential for the gamma-carboxylation of osteocalcin, a protein that binds calcium to the bone matrix. Inadequate vitamin K leads to under-carboxylated (inactive) osteocalcin and reduced bone mineralization. Low vitamin K intake is associated with increased fracture risk, and K2 supplementation shows benefits for bone density in some studies.

faqs.question: What is PIVKA-II?
faqs.answer: PIVKA-II (protein induced by vitamin K absence-II) is a non-functional form of prothrombin (des-gamma-carboxy prothrombin) produced when vitamin K is insufficient to gamma-carboxylate the prothrombin molecule during synthesis. It is detectable in the blood before PT becomes abnormal and is a specific marker of subclinical vitamin K deficiency.

faqs.question: Can vitamin K deficiency cause seizures in newborns?
faqs.answer: Vitamin K deficiency can cause intracranial hemorrhage in newborns, which can present with seizures. This is why the classic presentation of late-onset vitamin K deficiency bleeding (at 2-12 weeks) includes seizures, vomiting, and lethargy — signs of intracranial hemorrhage. Routine vitamin K prophylaxis at birth prevents this.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PCC and FFP?
faqs.answer: Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) is a concentrated, purified preparation of factors II, VII, IX, and X (the vitamin K-dependent factors) in a small volume for IV infusion. Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) is plasma from donated blood containing all clotting factors in a large volume. PCC acts faster, requires smaller volumes, and is more predictable than FFP — it has largely replaced FFP for urgent vitamin K deficiency bleeding.

metaTitle: Vitamin K Deficiency: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
metaDescription: Vitamin K deficiency: bleeding disorder from low clotting factors II, VII, IX, X. Easy bruising, prolonged PT/INR, mucosal bleeding. Oral/IV phytonadione treatment.
